perturbation.original_text:
The Executive agrees and undertakes with the Company acting on behalf of itself and as agent for each Group Company that he will not in any Relevant Capacity at any time during the Restricted Period:
(a) within or in relation to the Restricted Territory take any steps preparatory to or be directly or indirectly engaged, employed, interested or concerned in:
(i) any Competing Business; and/or
(ii) any Target Business Entity,
perturbation.changed_text:
The Executive will consider, in good faith, the Company's interests and undertakes with the Company acting on behalf of itself and as agent for each Group Company that he will endeavor not to be directly engaged, employed, interested or concerned in:
(a) within or in relation to the Restricted Territory taking preparatory steps for or being indirectly engaged, employed, interested or concerned in:
(i) any Competing Business; and/or
(ii) any Target Business Entity,
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees and undertakes that he will not' to 'will consider, in good faith, the Company's interests and undertakes with the Company that he will endeavor not to' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor not to' is less forceful than 'will not'. Also changing 'take any steps preparatory to or be directly or indirectly engaged' to 'taking preparatory steps for or being indirectly engaged' changes the meaning of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The modified text introduces ambiguities, potentially conflicting with UK law regarding restraint of trade, specifically the requirement for restrictive covenants to be clearly defined and reasonable to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_citation: Sections 4 and 5 of the Powers of Attorney Act 1971
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the obligation ambiguous, the modified text weakens the enforceability of the protective covenants. UK law requires such covenants to be clearly defined and reasonable in scope to be upheld in court. The ambiguity introduced makes it harder to prove a breach and to enforce the covenant, therefore contradicting the spirit and intention of UK law on restraint of trade. Restrictive covenants should be precisely drafted to give a clear indication of what is allowed and what is not. The UK courts will interpret any ambiguity in favour of the employee. Here, the alteration would create significant ambiguity as to what actions are prohibited, making it difficult to enforce the clause against the executive. In this contract, the Powers of Attorney Act 1971 is referenced, which has a section on irrevocability - something the new amendment would affect.

perturbation.justification: The original text states the executive 'will not' take certain actions, creating a definitive obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'will endeavor not to', introducing ambiguity. The Law Explanation states that UK law requires protective covenants to be 'clearly defined' and that courts will interpret ambiguity in favor of the employee. This suggests that the change weakens the enforceability of the clause. Additionally, the scraped snippet mentions the Powers of Attorney Act 1971, however there is no explanation or indication that this is relevant.

perturbation.original_text: The Executive shall, at the time of signing this Agreement, appoint the Company as his attorney by executing a Power of Attorney in the form set out in Schedule 1 so that the Company can give effect to the provisions of sub clause 17.3 above and clause 14 above as required.
perturbation.changed_text: The Executive is expected to consider appointing the Company as his attorney by executing a Power of Attorney in the form set out in Schedule 1 so that the Company can potentially give effect to the provisions of sub clause 17.3 above and clause 14 above.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'The Executive shall' to 'The Executive is expected to consider' weakens the obligation to appoint the company as his attorney. Changing 'can give effect as required' to 'can potentially give effect' makes it ambiguous if the change is effective. This undermines the power of attorney.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The modification weakens the enforceability of the power of attorney, potentially conflicting with the requirements for a valid and effective power of attorney under the Powers of Attorney Act 1971 in the UK.
perturbation.law_explanation: Because the document is signed in the UK and invokes the Powers of Attorney Act 1971, this change weakens that power of attorney significantly. Section 1 of the Powers of Attorney Act 1971 dictates requirements for the execution of powers of attorney and states that an instrument creating a power of attorney shall be signed and attested in a prescribed manner. By changing the language from 'shall' to 'is expected to consider' weakens the force of the clause and gives the Executive an opportunity to not follow the requirements of the prescribed act. Further, the Act requires that the power of attorney instrument be effective. By changing 'can give effect as required' to 'can potentially give effect' makes it ambiguous if the power of attorney would be effective, or merely potentially effective.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Make, register or end a lasting power of attorney: Overview - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Make, register or end a lasting power of attorney Skip contents Overview A lasting power of attorney ( LPA ) is a legal document that lets you (the ‘donor’) appoint one or more people (known as ‘attorneys’) to help you make decisions or to make decisions on your behalf. This gives you more control over what happens to you if you have an accident or an illness and cannot make your own decisions (you ‘lack mental capacity’). This guide is also available in Welsh (Cymraeg) . There are 2 types of LPA : health and welfare property and financial affairs You can choose to make one type or both. Your LPA will be legally binding only in England and Wales. There’s a different way to make a power of attorney if you need one that: works in Scotland works in Northern Ireland Eligibility You must be 18 or over and have mental capacity (the ability to make your own decisions) when you make your LPA . You do not need to live in the UK or be a British citizen to make an LPA . How to make a lasting power of attorney Choose your attorney (you can have more than one). Fill in the forms to appoint them as an attorney . Register your LPA with the Office of the Public Guardian (this will take 8 to 10 weeks if there are no mistakes in the application). Applying to register an LPA costs £82 unless you get a reduction or exemption . You can cancel your LPA if you no longer need it or want to make a new one. Health and welfare lasting power of attorney Use this LPA to give an attorney the power to make decisions about things like: your daily routine, for example washing, dressing, eating medical care moving into a care home life-sustaining treatment It can only be used when you’re unable to make your own decisions. Property and financial affairs lasting power of attorney Use this LPA to give an attorney the power to make decisions about money and property for you, for example: managing a bank or building society account paying bills collecting benefits or a pension selling your home It can be used as soon as it’s registered, with your permission. Help deciding if you should make a lasting power of attorney Contact the Office of the Public Guardian if you need help. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Executive to appoint the Company as his attorney, while the changed text weakens this obligation to 'is expected to consider,' creating ambiguity about whether the appointment will actually occur. Furthermore, the original text states the company 'can give effect as required', while the changed text says 'can potentially give effect' which makes the effectiveness of the power of attorney uncertain. According to the LLM's Law Explanation, this directly undermines the effectiveness of the power of attorney, invoking the Powers of Attorney Act 1971. The provided text from GOV.UK regarding lasting power of attorney reinforces the idea that the document should 'give an attorney the power to make decisions' and 'gives you more control over what happens to you'. By making the action of appointing the company as an attorney merely a consideration, the contract introduces an ambiguity that weakens the legally binding nature of the original obligation. The changes create an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, which falls under the provided definition. This is a contradiction as the change makes the power of attorney less binding and less effective, which contradicts its purpose.
